label: Glucosamine can be Hydrolysed from Chitin
title: Chitin, a natural polymer found in Crustacean Shells such as Shrimp and Crabs, can be chemically or enzymatically broken down and deacetylated to produce Glucosamine, a compound widely used in Joint Health Supplements.

label: Glucosamine can be Fermented from Corn
title: Glucosamine can also be produced from Corn through microbial fermentation, where corn-derived Glucose is converted into Glucosamine. This method provides a non–shellfish alternative often marketed as suitable for vegans and people with shellfish allergies.

label: Collagen (Type I, V, X) can be Extracted from Eggshell Membrane
title: Eggshell Membrane (ESM) is a natural, sustainable, vegetarian-friendly source of Collagen Type I, V, X and other joint-supporting compounds. ESM is becoming a more popular alternative source for Collagen to Bovine and Marine sources.

label: Chondroitin can be Hydrolysed from Animal Cartilage
title: Animal Cartilage from land animals such as Cattle and Pigs is commonly processed through enzymatic hydrolysis and purification to extract Chondroitin Sulfate, a compound widely used in Joint Health Supplements.

label: Chondroitin can be Hydrolysed from Shark Cartilage
title: Shark Cartilage is a traditional source of Chondroitin Sulfate, obtained through multi-step extraction and purification processes. It has been widely used in supplements, though its sourcing raises conservation and sustainability concerns.

label: Derivative
title: Gelatin is produced by breaking down Collagen (primarily type I) from the skins/hides, bones, and connective tissues of Pigs, Cattle, or Avian sources through heat and controlled processing, converting it into a soluble, gel-forming protein.

label: Collagen Type I & III can be Extracted from Bovine Hide
title: Animal Skins and Hides, rich in Type I Collagen, are washed, defatted, and treated with acid or alkali to break down cellular structures, allowing Collagen to be extracted and further processed.

label: Collagen Type I & III can be Extracted from Bovine Connective Tissues (Tendons, Ligaments, and Fascia)
title: Dense Connective Tissues such as Tendons, Ligaments, and Fascia contain high levels of Type I Collagen. These tissues are mechanically and enzymatically processed to extract Collagen for nutraceutical and biomedical uses.

label: Collagen Type I & III can be Extracted from Porcine Bones
title: Collagen extracted from Porcine Bones is primarily Type I Collagen, obtained after cleaning, demineralisation, and controlled processing. It is commonly used to make Gelatin, Collagen Peptides, food ingredients, and nutraceutical products.

label: Collagen Type I & III can be Extracted from Avian Skin
title: Avian Skins, especially Chicken, contain Type I and III Collagen, processed into Broth Products, Collagen Peptides, and limited Cosmetic and Food Applications.

label: Collagen Type I & III can be Extracted from Pig Skin
title: Pig Skin is a major commercial source of Type I Collagen. It is widely processed into Food-Grade Collagen, Gelatin, Sausage Casings, and cosmetic or pharmaceutical collagen products.

label: Collagen Type I & III can be Extracted from Porcine Connective Tissues (Tendons, Ligaments, and Fascia)
title: Porcine Connective Tissues, including tendons and fascia, are rich in Type I Collagen and are processed into Collagen Peptides, Joint Health Supplements, and functional food ingredients.

label: Collagen Type I & III can be Extracted from Animal Cartilage
title: Animal Cartilage, which is rich in Type II Collagen, is separated from Bones and other Connective Tissues, then enzymatically or chemically processed to extract Collagen for use in Joint Health Supplements and biomedical applications.

label: Collagen Type I & III can be Extracted from Bovine Bones
title: Animal Bones are cleaned and demineralised using acid or alkali treatments to release Type I Collagen, which can then be further processed into Collagen or Gelatin.

label: Marine Collagen (Type I) can be Extracted from Fish Scales & Skin
title: Marine Collagen is commonly extracted from Fish Scales and Skins, which are cleaned and treated with acid or enzymes to isolate Type I Collagen. These sources are widely used in Cosmetics, Wound Dressings, and skincare products due to their high bioavailability and marine origin.

label: Marine Collagen (Type I) can be Extracted from Fish Byproducts (heads, bones, fins, vertebrae, cartilage)
title: Marine Collagen is also extracted from Fish Byproducts such as heads, fins, bones, cartilage, and vertebrae. These materials are cooked or enzymatically processed to release Collagen, which is commonly used in Marine Collagen Powders, Collagen Peptides, and certain Pharmaceutical Products.

label: Marine Collagen (Type I) can be Extracted from Shark Cartilage
title: Shark Cartilage is a controversial source of Marine Collagen, often marketed as a high-value ingredient because it is rich in Type II Collagen, which is associated with joint and cartilage support.

label: MSM (Methylsulfonylmethane) can be Oxidised from DMSO (Dimethyl Sulfoxide)
title: MSM used in Dietary Supplements is typically synthesised by oxidising DMSO (Dimethyl Sulfoxide) through controlled chemical or electrochemical processes, producing a stable, bioavailable sulfur compound.

label: Hyaluronic Acid can be Extracted from Rooster Combs
title: Hyaluronic Acid (HA) has traditionally been extracted from connective tissues in Rooster Combs through purification and isolation processes, and was historically a primary commercial source.

label: Hyaluronic Acid can be Fermented from Bacteria (Streptococcus spp. or Bacillus spp.)
title: Today, Hyaluronic Acid is widely produced using Bacterial Fermentation, where selected Microorganisms synthesise HA in controlled bioreactors. This method is preferred for its scalability, purity, and suitability for Pharmaceuticals, Cosmetics, and Joint Health Supplements.

label: Surimi can be Processed from Fish Meat & Products
title: Surimi is made from Fish Meat, which is finely minced, repeatedly washed to remove fats and pigments, and blended with stabilisers to create a neutral-flavoured protein base used in products such as Crab Sticks and other seafood analogues.

label: Collagen (Type II) can be Extracted from Avian (Poultry) Bones
title: Poultry Bones (especially Chicken) are rich in Collagen Type II, commonly extracted from Sternum Bones and Cartilage for use in Joint Support Formulas.

label: Collagen (Type II) can be Extracted from Poultry Connective Tissues (Tendons, Ligaments, and Fascia)
title: Poultry Connective Tissues contain smaller amounts of Collagen (type II) and are less commonly used at industrial scale, but are still processed into Broths and Joint Support Formulas.

label: Contributes to
title: Runoff of nutrients, especially Nitrogen and Phosphate from soil Fertilisers, can enter lakes, estuaries, and other water bodies, leading to Eutrophication. (133)
Energy Overconsumption, largely linked to Energy Production, accounts for roughly 75% of global GHG Emissions. (136)

label: Drives
title: Energy Consumption is a primary driver of Climate Change, as its production accounts for the majority of Greenhouse Gas Emissions, mainly through the burning of fossil fuels. (136)
Soil Fertility Decline leads to chemical and biological deterioration of soil quality, directly contributing to Soil Degradation. (140)

label: Contributes to
title: Soil Fertility Decline and Soil Erosion reinforce each other: The loss of organic matter reduces soil structure and makes soils more vulnerable to erosion, while erosion removes nutrient-rich topsoil, further diminishing soil fertility. (149)
Pesticides and Herbicides contribute to Soil Degradation by killing beneficial soil microbes, disrupting nutrient cycles, damaging soil structure, and leaving persistent residues. (138)

label: Contributes to
title: Pesticides leach from fields into groundwater and surface water, elevating toxicity levels. (25, 122)

label: Leads to
title: Repeated use of Pesticides and Herbicides drives natural selection and revolutionary adaptation. (128)

label: Contributes to
title: Herbicide residues persist in soil, reducing microbial diversity and affecting soil health in fields. (25, 121)

label: Contributes to
title: Pesticides can enter air through evaporation, spray drift during application, and wind erosion of contaminated soil (25, 122)

label: Contributes to
title: Pesticides can kill off beneficial insects, pollinators, non-target species, and disrupt soil fauna (120)

label: Causes
title: Water Overuse, particularly in Agriculture, drives Ground Water Depletion by extracting water faster than natural recharge rates. (120)

label: Results in
title: Soil degradation weakens the soil's structure and reduces organic matter, making it more vulnerable to erosion by wind and water. (120, 124, 149)

label: Causes
title: Deforestation destroys habitats, causing widespread habitat loss and endangering many plant and animal species. (126,127)

label: Exacerbates
title: Deforestation worsens atmospheric carbon levels, as the loss of trees diminishes the capacity of forests to sequester CO2. (127)

label: Contributes to
title: Deforested areas lose the ability to regulate moisture and water cycles, altering weather patterns and contributing to Climate Change. (127)

label: Leads to
title: Deforestation leads to Soil Erosion as the loss of trees reduces water retention and destabilizes topsoil, particularly in coastal areas. (127)

label: Contributes to
title: Forests sustain the livelihoods of millions of people, communities, and national economies. Deforestation diminishes these resources, indirectly affecting the economy. (126)
Monoculture Farming drives Excessive Fertilisation, as repeatedly growing the same crop exhausts soil nutrients and degrades the soil microbiome. (125)
Monoculture Farming causes significant Soil Fertility Decline by depleting nutrients and reducing beneficial microbes and organisms that maintain soil health. (141)
Monoculture Farming typically requires higher Pesticide & Herbicide Use because single-crop systems are more vulnerable to pests and disease outbreaks. (125)
Monoculture Farming increases Water Overuse because uniform root systems reduce soil structure, water uptake, and water retention. (125)

Monoculture Farming disrupts soil balance by depleting nutrients, reducing fertility, and accelerating Soil Erosion, as a single crop root system cannot adequately retain water or stabilize soil. (125)

label: Contributes to
title: Monoculture reduces Biodiversity and leads to a less resilient and poorer ecosystem. (125)

label: Results in
title: Expansion of monocultures converts natural ecosystems, reducing habitat connectivity for wildlife. (120)

label: Causes
title: Greenhouse Gases such as Carbon Dioxide, Methane, Nitrous Oxide, and certain synthetic chemicals trap outgoing heat in the atmosphere, altering climate and weather patterns. (135)
Water Contamination with excess nutrients such as Nitrogen and Phosphorus leads to Eutrophication, promoting Algal Blooms and Dead Zones (oxygen depletion). (133)

label: Results in
title: Contaminated Water from human activities introduces microbes, chemicals, heavy metals, and radioactive elements into drinking water sources such as rivers, groundwater, and tanks, making it unsafe without proper treatment. (142)

label: Drives
title: Water Contamination harms both aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems. Pollutants directly damage aquatic life and can infiltrate soils, altering their properties and harming plants, creating cascading effects that can lead to Ecosystem Degradation or even Collapse. (144)

label: Results in
title: Water Contamination not only threatens drinking water, but also harms human health through non-drinking pathways, such as skin contact with polluted water and inhalation of contaminated droplets or aerosols. (142, 143)

label: Triggers
title: Eutrophication promotes rapid algal growth, which can include toxin-producing species that lead to Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs). (145)

label: Causes
title: Eutrophication (nutrient overload in water) acts as a fertiliser, allowing algae to grow and reproduce rapidly and form dense blooms. (145)
Soil Contaminants, including Pesticides, Fertilisers, and Heavy Metals, can leach into groundwater and flow into surface waters. (130)
Runoff of nutrients—particularly Nitrogen and Phosphorus from Fertilisers—can enter lakes, estuaries, and coastal waters, triggering Eutrophication. (133)
Pollutants released into the air—such as Sulfur, Nitrogen, and Heavy Metals—can enter rivers, lakes, and oceans through rain, snow, or airborne dust. (129)
Atmospheric deposition of Nitrogen adds excess nutrients to the Marine Environment, contributing to Eutrophication. (132)
Air Pollutants, especially Carbon Dioxide, increase acidity and lower pH levels in lakes, streams, and the ocean, altering habitats for aquatic flora and fauna. (129,131)

label: Leads to
title: Biodiversity Loss reduces essential ecosystem services such as pollination, water purification, flood control, and soil fertility, creating cascading effects that can lead to Ecosystem Degradation or even Ecosystem Collapse. (147)
Habitat Loss and Fragmentation isolate populations and reduce available living space, increasing local extinctions and driving Biodiversity Loss. (134)

label: Top Consumer & Top Importer & Significant Producer
title: U.S. is the largest Consumer market for Joint Health Supplements (3). U.S. is the largest Importer of Joint Health Supplements (~33% of global shipments) (3). U.S. is a notable Producer of Joint Health Supplements (48, 67)

label: Top Producer
title: The US is a major Producer of Gummies, including both flavored confectionery and dietary supplement varieties (48, 107, 108)

label: Significant Consumer
title: The EU is a notable Consumer of Joint Health Supplements, ~20% of global market share (5)

label: Significant Exporter
title: Australia is a major Exporter of Joint Health Supplements (67)

label: Significant Producer
title: Germany is a traditional hub for confectionery production and a major Producer of gummy candies, best known for the brand Haribo (48, 107, 108)

label: Top Producer & Top Exporter
title: China is the largest Supplier and Manufacturer of Joint Health Supplements, ~80% of global production (1). China is the largest Exporter of Joint Health Supplements, also largest share in US imports (~77%) (2)

label: Top Producer
title: China is one of the largest Producers of gummies globally, leading in both flavored confectionery and dietary supplement varieties (48, 107, 108)

label: Significant Consumer & Significant Producer
title: Japan is a significant Consumer of Joint Health Supplements due to aging population (2). Japan is a major Producer of Joint Health Supplements in Asia-Pacific (2)

label: Recognised Producer
title: Japan is a notable Producer of Gummies, known for unique flavors and textures, producing both confectionery and dietary supplement varieties (48, 107)

label: Significant Producer & Significant Exporter
title: India is a major Producer and Exporter of Joint Health Supplements (4). India is a major Exporter of Joint Health Supplements (48, 67)

label: Significant Importer
title: Vietnam is a significant Importer of Joint Health Supplements (~18% of global shipments) (3, 67)

label: Global Leader & Top Exporter
title: The US has the largest Corn Farming industry, producing ~354.19M tonnes, with ~57.79M tonnes exported (2022-2023) (9, 38). The US is the largest Exporter of Corn, ~US$14.3B, 2024 (8)

label: Significant Grower
title: The US has a major Cattle Farming industry, with a large herd (~92.08M, 2022) and leading Beef Production despite a smaller head count than India, Brazil, and China (50)

label: Significant Processor
title: The US ranks among the top 5 in annual Cattle Slaughter, ~30.9M head (2025) (96)

label: Significant Grower
title: The US has a significant Pig Farming industry, ranking among the top producers globally, though far behind China, with a pig population of ~74.5M (2021-2025) (94, 95)

label: Recognised Processor
title: The US ranks among the top in Swine Slaughter, after China and the EU, with ~131.8M pigs killed/year (97)

label: Top Importer
title: The US is the leading Importer of Live Bovines by both value and head count (~$2.99B, ~2M head, 2024) (84)

label: Recognised Importer
title: The US, along with Spain, is a significant Livestock Bovine Importer (~$208.75M, ~4.88M head, 2024) (85)

label: Significant Exporter
title: The United States is a notable Exporter of live poultry/fowls—including chickens, ducks, geese, turkeys, and guinea fowls—ranking high by trade value (~$359.62M) with a lower export quantity (~81.75M head, 2024) (86, 87, 88, 89)

label: Significant Processor
title: The US ranks 2nd in Poultry Slaughter after China, with ~9.75B fowls/year (2023), incl. chickens, turkeys, ducks, though far behind in number (98)

label: Significant Catcher
title: The US has well-established lobster and shrimp fisheries (43)

label: Global Leader
title: The USA is the undisputed global leader in Biotechnology, leading in both firm numbers and R&D (48, 73, 74)

label: Significant Producer
title: The US is among the top global shrimp producers with ~837,622 tonnes (40, 41)

label: Significant Catcher
title: Indonesia ranks 2nd globally in Fisheries production, with 7.4M tonnes in 2022, about half of China's (75)

label: Top Catcher
title: Indonesia is the undisputed leader in Shark Fishing, consistently ranking first in global shark catch across years and reports (48, 76, 77, 78)

label: Significant Player
title: Indonesia has a growing Crustacean Processing industry, notably frozen and prepared shrimps and prawns, with exports valued at $1.35 billion (39, 81, 82)

label: Significant Processor
title: Indonesia ranks in the top 5 for Poultry Slaughter, with ~4.96B fowls/year (incl. chickens, ducks; 2023) (98)

label: Significant Catcher
title: Indonesia is a major crustacean catcher, with coastal and small-scale fisheries yielding significant shrimp and crab catches (43)

label: Significant Grower
title: Indonesia is a notable and emerging producer of mud and soft-shell crabs (44)

label: Significant Grower
title: The EU has a significant Corn Farming industry, producing ~58M tonnes, but remains below domestic demand of ~77.4M tonnes (2022-2023) (38)

label: Recognised Processor
title: The EU is a notable processor in Cattle Slaughter, ~22.5M cows killed/year (2025) (96)

label: Recognised Processor
title: The EU slaughters ~220M pigs/year—ranking 2nd globally in Swine Slaughter, though far behind China (2025) (97)

label: Significant Exporter
title: The EU is a notable Livestock Bovine Exporter by value, along with Brazil (~$837M, ~615K head, 2024) (83)

label: Top Exporter
title: The EU is the leading Exporter of young chickens (chicks) by value and head count, ~$349.18M, ~185.3M (86)

label: Significant Grower & Top Exporter
title: Brazil has the 3rd largest Corn Farming industry, after the US and China, producing ~136M tonnes and exporting ~47M tonnes (2022-2023) (9, 38). Brazil is the second largest exporter of Corn, ~US$8.2B, 2024 (8)

label: Significant Grower
title: Brazil has the second-largest cattle herd (~193.78M, 2022), ranking 2nd in both beef production and Cattle Farming industry (50)

label: Top Processor
title: Brazil is one of the leading countries in Cattle Slaughter after China, with ~47.5M head/year (2025) (96)

label: Significant Grower
title: Brazil is a significant grower in Pig Farming, ranking after the US with a pig population of ~44.4M (2021-2025) (94, 95)

label: Significant Exporter
title: Brazil is a notable Livestock Bovine Exporter by value, alongside the EU (~$848M, 2024) (83)

label: Significant Processor
title: Brazil is among the top countries in Poultry Slaughter after China and the US, with ~6.30B fowls/year (incl. chickens, turkeys; 2023) (98)

label: Significant Grower & Significant Exporter
title: Argentina is the 2nd largest Corn producer in South America after Brazil and is one of the top global producers with production ~ 55M tonnes (8, 38). Argentina is a significant Exporter of Corn, ~US$6.6B, 2024 (8)

label: Recognised Catcher
title: Argentina is a notable Shark-Catching nation in the South Atlantic, with both targeted and incidental catches (48, 76, 77)

label: Significant Exporter
title: Ukraine is within the top 5 Exporters of Corn, ~US$5.1B, 2024 (8)

label: Significant Grower
title: The Philippines is a notable producer of blue swimming crabs, primarily for export (44)

label: Top Player

label: Significant Catcher

label: Recognised Grower

label: Significant Importer

label: Recognised Importer
title: Spain, along with the US, is a significant Livestock Bovine Importer (~$333.6M, ~3.7M head, 2024) (85)

label: Significant Catcher
title: Taiwan is a major Shark-Catching nation, with targeted longline fisheries harvesting a wide range of shark species (48, 76, 77)

label: Significant Player

label: Significant Player
title: Switzerland is a significant player in Biotechnology, leading in R&D gloabally and amongst the top 10 by market share (48, 73, 74)

label: Significant Importer

label: Significant Player
title: Belgium is a notable biotech hub, close to Switzerland in R&D intensity (48, 73, 74)

label: Significant Player

The Netherlands is the leading Importer of live poultry/fowls—including young and mature chickens, ducks, geese, turkeys, and guinea fowls—by both trade value (~$972.52M) and quantity (~359.91M head, 2024) (90, 91, 92, 93)

label: Top Exporter

Unsafe Drinking Water can cause short- and long-term health effects, ranging from gastrointestinal illness to cancer, depending on the contaminants. Chemical Exposure may lead to skin conditions, nervous system or organ damage, developmental or reproductive disorders, and cancer, while Microbial Contamination can spread diseases such as cholera, typhoid, hepatitis, and severe diarrheal illness. (142)
Harmful Algal Blooms can create Hypoxia and Dead Zones when decaying algae consume dissolved oxygen in the water. (145)
Harmful Algal Blooms, often involving toxin-producing cyanobacteria in freshwater or dinoflagellates in marine environments, release poisons that are lethal to aquatic wildlife. (146)
Toxins from Harmful Algal Blooms can poison wildlife and deplete oxygen in surrounding waters, destabilizing ecosystems and contributing to Ecosystem Degradation. (145)
Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs) can affect human health through inhalation of toxin-containing aerosols released by certain algal species. (143)
Hypoxia/Dead Zones directly causes Aquatic Life Loss, as low-oxygen conditions cannot support most fish and invertebrates. (146)
Algal Blooms may include or promote toxin-producing species, but not all blooms develop into Harmful Algal Blooms. (145)
Algal Blooms are a major driver of Hypoxia; when algae die and decompose, oxygen levels drop, forming Dead Zones. (145)
Even non-toxic Algal Blooms can block sunlight, damage fish gills, and disrupt ecosystems, leading to Aquatic Life Loss. (145)
Ecosystem Degradation and Collapse increase the risk of Species Extinction, as the loss of key species triggers cascading failures across ecosystems. (143)

label: Associates with
title: Excessive nitrogen and phosphorus fertilizers applied to boost yields in Corn Farming result in nutrient imbalances and runoff risks. (119)

label: Associates with
title: Intensive application of herbicides like atrazine and sulfonylureas in Corn Farming leads to persistent residues in soil, affecting crop rotation and non-target organisms. (118)

label: Associates with
title: Corn is one of the most widely grown crops globally, with about 33% of production occurring in highly water-stressed areas, raising concerns about water supply and long-term sustainability. (148)

label: Associates with
title: Tillage and monocropping in Corn Farming erode topsoil and deplete organic matter, harming soil structure. (120)

label: Contributes to
title: Rising global demand for Corn, primarily fueled by the Livestock and Ethanol industries, is driving land expansion, which often leads to Deforestation, especially in Brazil, one of the world's largest Corn Producers. (123)

label: Associates with
title: Corn Farming is often practiced as a monoculture, especially in large-scale, industrialized agriculture. ()
Cattle Farming supplies live cattle to the Cattle Slaughtering industry, where animals are processed into meat and byproducts.
Cattle Farming produces live Cattle that are traded domestically and internationally for Breeding or Slaughter.
Rendering Plants process raw byproducts from Cattle Slaughtering—including Bones, Cartilage, Hides, Fat, Offal, Blood, Hooves, and Horns—into usable materials.
Farmed Pigs are transported to Slaughtering facilities for killing and initial processing
Pig Farming produces pigs that are widely traded for Breeding, Fattening, or Slaughter.
Rendering Plants collect and process byproducts from Pig Slaughtering, such as Bones, Cartilage, Fat, Offal, and Blood, into industrial ingredients.
The Animal Rendering Industry supplies prepared Animal Skins and Hides to the Tanning Industry for leather production.

The Animal Rendering Industry supplies the Pharmaceuticals Industry with materials such as Collagen, Gelatin, Chondroitin Sulfate, Heparin, and Insulin, derived from Bovine, Porcine, and Avian byproducts.

The Rendering Industry provides the Cosmetics Industry with ingredients including Animal Fats, Gelatin, Collagen, Keratin, Glycerin (Glycerol), and Stearic Acid, used as emollients, thickeners, and active components in personal care products.
Poultry Farming produces birds traded domestically and internationally for Breeding, Egg Production, Fattening, or Slaughter.
Farmed Avian species are supplied to the Poultry Slaughtering industry for killing and initial carcass processing.
Crustacean Fisheries supply a major share of raw material to Crustacean Processing, contributing roughly 4–6 million tons of wild-caught crustaceans each year. (34)
Shrimp and Prawn Farming supplies around 4 million tons of farmed shrimp annually; together, farmed and wild shrimp dominate Crustacean Processing volumes. (31, 34)
Lobster Farming contributes a small but growing share of inputs to Crustacean Processing, remaining minor compared with wild catch. (36)
Crab Farming is an expanding aquaculture segment that provides a minor share of raw material for Crustacean Processing. (37)
Crayfish Farming supplies a small but growing portion of inputs to Crustacean Processing, with volumes well below Shrimp and Crab. (34)
Byproducts from Poultry Slaughtering are processed by the Rendering Industry into usable materials, though at much smaller volumes than Bovine or Porcine sources.
The Forestry Industry supplies Wood as the primary raw material for Paper Manufacturing.

label: Crustacean Shells is a Byproduct of Crustacean Processing
title: The Crustacean Processing industry is the primary source of shells, particularly from shrimp, crabs, and lobsters, processing 6-8 million tons annually, 50-70% of crustacean weight are discarded as shells, cephalothoraxes, and exoskeletons (30, 31, 34)

label: Crustacean Shells is a Byproduct of Crustacean Fisheries
title: Crustacean Fisheries are a major source of shells, especially from wild-caught shrimp, crabs, and lobsters (48)

label: Crustacean Shells is a Byproduct of Shrimp and Prawn Farming
title: Shrimp and Prawn Farming contribute significantly to shell supply due to high production (40, 41)

label: Crustacean Shells is a Byproduct of Lobster Farming
title: Lobster Farming is a minor shell source compared to other crustaceans (36)

label: Crustacean Shells is a Byproduct of Crab Farming
title: Crab Farming contributes moderately to shell supply, though volumes are lower than shrimp or prawn (44)

label: Crustacean Shells is a Byproduct of Crayfish Farming
title: Crayfish Farming is a minor contributor to shell supply at lower volumes than shrimp and crab (47)

label: Wild-Caught Fish is a Target Catch of Commercial Fishing
title: Wild-Caught Fish are harvested through Commercial Fishing and Artisanal Fishing primarily for human consumption, common species including Cod, Tuna, Anchovy, Herring, and Mackerel

label: Wild-Caught Fish is a Target Catch of Artisanal Fishing
title: Yields of WIld-Caught Fish from Artisanal Fishing are much smaller than Commercial Fishing, primarily for local consumption, catches are less likely to be processed into byproducts

label: Eggs is a Main Product of Layer Poultry Farming/Egg Production
title: Layer Poultry Farming is a specialised branch of Poultry Farming focused on breeding and raising Chickens for Egg Production.

label: Direct product of
title: Laying Hens, primarily White Leghorn in commercial Egg Production, can produce around 290 to 300 Eggs per year.

label: Filamentuous Fungi is a Main Product of Biotechnology
title: Filamentous Fungi is cultivated at industrial-scale in the Biotechnology industry by selecting or engineering suitable fungal strains in lab-controlled environments

label: Bovine (Cow) is a Primary Product of Cattle Farming
title: Bovine refers to domesticated Cattle raised in the Cattle Farming Industry for meat, milk, breeding, and other purposes

label: Bovine (Cow) is a Raw Material of Cattle Slaughtering
title: The Cattle Slaughtering Industry processes Bovines for meat as the primary product before further processing in downstream industries

label: Bovine (Cow) is a Raw Material of Tanning Industry
title: Bovine is the primary raw material for the Leather Processing industry, whose products include leather, gelatin, collagen, and related materials

label: Bovine (Cow) is a Raw Material of Livestock Trading (Bovine)
title: Live Bovines are traded in the Livestock Trade industry for breeding, fattening, or slaughter

label: Bovine (Cow) is a Raw Material of Rendering (Animals)
title: Non-meat parts of Bovine are sent to the Rendering Industry for processing

label: Bovine (Cow) is a Source of Pharmaceuticals Industry
title: Bovine byproducts are an important source for the Pharmaceutical Industry, used in the production of Heparin, Insulin, Collagen, Gelatin, and other medical materials

label: Bovine (Cow) is a Source of Pet Food Industry
title: Bovine products, byproducts, and derivatives—such as meat trimmings, organs, fat, bone meal, and tallow—are used in the Pet Food Industry as sources of protein, nutrients, and energy

label: Bovine (Cow) is a Source of Cosmetics Industry
title: Bovine byproducts and derivatives—such as Tallow, Collagen, Gelatin, Keratin, and Glycerin—are widely used in the Cosmetics Industry as emollients, stabilisers, and active ingredients in skincare and personal care products

label: Sharks and Elasmobranchs is a Bycatch of Fisheries
title: Sharks and Elasmobranchs (e.g. rays, skates) are unintentionally caught alongside target species during commercial or artisanal fishing operations

label: Sharks and Elasmobranchs is a Target Catch of Shark Fisheries
title: Sharks and Elasmobranchs (e.g. rays, skates) are deliberately caught in targeted fisheries for their meat, fins, cartilage, or other parts

label: Porcine (Pig) is a Primary Product of Pig Farming
title: Porcine refers to domesticated Pigs raised in the Pig Farming Industry for meat production, breeding, and related uses

label: Porcine (Pig) is a Primary Product of Pig Slaughtering
title: The Pig Slaughtering Industry processes Pigs for meat as the primary product before further processing in downstream industries

label: Porcine (Pig) is a Primary Product of Livestock Trading (Porcine)
title: Live Porcine Animals are traded across markets for breeding, fattening, or slaughter

label: Porcine (Pig) is a Raw Material of Rendering (Animals)
title: Non-meat parts from Porcine are sent to the Rendering Industry for processing

label: Porcine (Pig) is a Source of Pharmaceuticals Industry
title: Porcine byproducts—such as intestinal mucosa, glands, and tissues—are key sources for the Pharmaceutical Industry, used in the production of Heparin, Insulin, Gelatin, and other medical products

label: Porcine (Pig) is a Source of Pet Food Industry
title: Porcine products, byproducts, and derivatives such as meat trimmings, organs, fat, and rendered meals are widely used in the Pet Food industry as essential protein, fat, and nutrient sources

label: Porcine (Pig) is a Source of Cosmetics Industry
title: Porcine byproducts and derivatives—such as Collagen, Gelatin, Fat, and Glycerin—are used in the Cosmetics Industry for their moisturizing, texturizing, and stabilising properties in skincare and personal care products

label: Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls) is a Raw Material of Rendering (Animals)
title: Avian slaughter byproducts are sent to the Rendering industry but in much smaller volumes than Bovine and Porcine, contributing minimally to downstream industries like Pharmaceuticals and Cosmetics

label: Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls) is a Source of Pet Food Industry
title: Avian meat, byproducts, and derivatives are used in the Pet Food industry as protein and nutrient sources

label: Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls) is a Primary Product of Poultry Slaughtering
title: Avian species are sent to the Slaughtering industry for meat production as the primary output

label: Farmed Fish is a Primary Product of Fish Aquaculture
title: Fish Aquaculture produces Farmed Fish primarily for human consumption, commonly Carp, Tilapia, Catfish, Salmon, and Pangasius

label: Dairy Cows is a Primary Product of Dairy Farming
title: Dairy cows are raised in Dairy Farming to produce milk and related dairy products

label: Lignin (Wood) is a Byproduct of Paper Manufacturing
title: Lignin is extracted as a byproduct during wood processing in the forestry industry, primarily from pulp and paper manufacturing

label: Global Leader

  (48, 53, 54)

label: Significant Producer & Recognised Exporter
title: China has significant Poultry Production, ~17-20M metric tonnes, ~16.6% of global production (48, 57). China is rapidly expanding Poultry Exports, particularly in processed chicken, ~0.8-1M tonnes (57, 58, 59)

label: Top Producer & Top Exporter
title: China is the world's largest Fish Producer, ~67.8M tonnes, 2024 (113). China is the largest Exporter of Farmed Fish in the world, with a value of ~$20.18 B in 2024 (112)

label: Top Importer & Top Consumer
title: China is amongst the top 3 importers of Chitin, while also being the largest producer and exporter (6). China is a leading consumer market for Chitin, driven by the increasing demands in agriculture, water treatment, and pharmaceuticals (48, 68)

label: Top Importer & Top Consumer
title: China is the 2nd largest Importer and reporter of Fish and other fishery products with import value ~$22.3B in 2024 (112). China is the largest Consumer of Fish and seafood, with ~59.3M tonnes in 2022 (114)

label: Significant Importer & Significant Consumer
title: China is a significant importer of Corn despite being a top producer itself due to high domestic demands, mainly for livestock feed and biofuels (11). China is the second largest consumer of Corn after the US; consumption often exceeds production, driving import demand (15)

label: Global Leader
title: China has the largest Shark Cartilage product market share, sales revenue ~$22.324M (2025) (66)

label: Top Consumer & Top Importer
title: China is closely behind the US in Beef Consumption (~11.6M tonnes, 2025), with demand continuing to rise (48, 49). China is the largest Beef Importer despite having a large bovine farming industry (~3.8M tonnes, 2025), driven by high domestic demand (48, 49)

label: Significant Importer & Top Consumer
title: China is one of the top Pork Importers due to strong domestic demand, despite being the world's largest producer (48, 53). China is the world's largest Pork Consumer, accounting for nearly half of global consumption (48, 53)

label: Top Consumer & Significant Importer
title: China is the second-largest Poultry Consumer after the US, with the fastest growth in consumption, ~ 15M tonnes, 2023 (57). China is a notable Poultry Importer after Japan, Mexico, and the UK, with imports of ~0.7M tonnes (48, 57)

label: Significant Exporter
title: Norway is the 2nd largest Exporter of aquatic animals (mostly farmed salmon), but also including Wild-Caught Fish such as cod, herring, mackerel, and some other whitefish and small pelagic species (117)

label: Significant Exporter
title: Norway is the second largest Exporter of Farmed Fish after China, primarily cultured Atlantic Salmon, with a value of ~$16 B in 2024 (112)

label: Recognised Exporter
title: Chile is a recognised Exporter of Farmed Fish, behind China, Norway, Ecuador, and Vietnam, with a value of ~$7.8 B in 2024 (112)

label: Top Importer & Significant Consumer
title: Mexico is the largest importer of Corn, driven by food and feed demand, imports mostly from the U.S. (8). Mexico is a large consumer of Corn, primarily for food and feed, substantial portion of supply imported primarily from the U.S. (16)

label: Significant Player
title: Mexico has significant market share of Shark Cartilage product, ~$9.241M sales revenue (2025) (66)

label: Significant Importer
title: Mexico is a fast-growing Pork Importer, driven by strong domestic demand (48, 53)

label: Top Importer
title: Mexico is close to Japan as a top Poultry Importer, with imports of ~0.8 to 1.01M tonnes (48, 57)

label: Significant Consumer & Significant Importer
title: Japan has high Chitin consumption primarily for biomedical applications (48, 68). Japan is a major importer of Chitin for uses in biomedical and agricultural applications (6)

label: Significant Importer & Recognised Consumer
title: Japan is the 3rd largest Importer of Fish after the US and China, valued at ~$12.9 B in 2024 (112). Japan is a recognised Consumer of Fish and seafood, behind China, India, Indonesia, and the US, with ~5.6 M tonnes in 2022 (114)

label: Significant Importer
title: Japan is a major Corn importer, mainly for animal feed (8)

label: Recognised Importer
title: Japan is a significant Beef Importer (~720K tonnes, 2025) with high demand due to strong consumption and limited local production (48, 49)

label: Top Importer
title: Japan is the world’s largest Importer of Pork (48, 53, 56)

label: Top Importer
title: Japan leads global Poultry Imports, totaling around 0.9–1.08M tonnes (48, 57)

label: Major Source & Significant Exporter
title: India is an emerging seafood Producer and Shell product supplier—incl. Chitin—with growing applications in agriculture and cosmetics (7, 17, 19, 79, 81). India is a notable Chitin Exporter, a compound derived directly from Crustacean Shells, likely ranking 2nd globally (6, 7)

label: Recognised Catcher
title: India is a recognised Catcher of Wild-Caught Fish alongside Peru, with ~5.5M tonnes in 2020 (116)

label: Recognised Producer & Significant Exporter
title: India is an emerging Bovine Producer (Beef), ~4.35M tonnes (2022), mainly of buffalo due to religious restrictions, generating various co- and by-products (48, 49, 50). India ranks among the top 5 Bovine Meat/Carabeef Exporters, mainly Buffalo meat, with ~1.4–1.6M tonnes (2024–2025) (48, 49, 51, 52)

label: Significant Producer
title: India is among the top three Producers of Farmed Fish after China and Indonesia, with ~10.9 M tonnes in 2021 (113, 115)

label: Significant Consumer
title: India is the 2nd largest Consumer of Fish and seafood, with ~12.6M tonnes in 2022 (114)

label: Significant Player
title: India has significant market share of Shark Cartilage product, ~$9.489M sales revenue (2025) (66)

label: Major Source
title: Thailand is one of the top Shrimp and prawn producers-incl. processed products, enabling abundant Crustacean Shell availability (17, 81)

label: Recognised Exporter
title: Thailand is an emerging Exporter of Poultry, supplying high-quality products to Asian markets, ~1.1-1.5M tonnes (57, 58, 59)

label: Significant Producer & Top Exporter
title: Vietnam is a significant Producer of Crustacean Shells (mainly Shrimp) and their derivative, Chitin, supported by its major Shrimp and Prawn Farming industry (6, 17, 81). Vietnam is one of the top Exporters of Chitin, derived from Crustacean Shells, primarily supplying regional markets in Asia (6, 48)

label: Recognised Exporter
title: Vietnam is a recognised Exporter of Wild-Caught Fish (Tuna), and the 3rd largest Exporter of aquatic animal products (incl. farmed fish and shrimps) (117)

label: Recognised Producer & Recognised Exporter
title: Vietnam is a recognised Producer of Farmed Fish, alongside Bangladesh, ranking after the top three (China, Indonesia, India), with ~6.4 M tonnes in 2021 (113, 115). Vietnam is a recognised Exporter of Farmed Fish, behind China, Norway, and Ecuador, with a value of ~$8.3 B in 2024 (112)

label: Significant Importer
title: Vietnam imports a significant quantity of Chitin despite being a notable producer to meet domestic demands (6)

label: Significant Importer
title: Vietnam is a notable importer of Corn, driven by feed and food processing (13)

label: Chitin can be Extracted from Crustacean Shells
title: Chitin is commonly extracted from Crustacean Shells—such as those of shrimp, crabs, and lobsters—through demineralisation (removal of calcium carbonate) and deproteinisation, leaving behind the chitin-rich structural material. (32, 33)

label: Chitin can be Extracted from Filamentuous Fungi
title: Chitin can also be extracted from the cell walls of Filamentous Fungi, offering a non-animal alternative source that is increasingly used in industrial, biomedical, and dietary applications. (35)

label: Bovine Hide can be Harvested from Bovine (Cow)
title: Bovine Hides are obtained from Cows/Cattle and are a primary byproduct of the Beef and Dairy industries.

label: Bovine Connective Tissues (Tendons, Ligaments, and Fascia) can be Harvested from Bovine (Cow)
title: Bovine Connective Tissues are harvested from Cattle during Slaughter and Meat Processing as high-collagen byproducts.

label: Fish Scales & Skin can be Harvested from Wild-Caught Fish
title: Fish Skins & Scales from Wild-Caught Fish (often species like Cod, Tilapia, and Sardines), are collected as Byproducts for further processing

label: Fish Scales & Skin can be Harvested from Farmed Fish
title: Fish Skin and Scales (esp. from tilapia, cod, carp) are lightweight and high in Type I Collagen. They’re processed into Marine Collagen, Biofilms, Wound Dressings, and cosmetic actives

label: Fish Byproducts (heads, bones, fins, vertebrae, cartilage) can be Harvested from Wild-Caught Fish
title: Other Fish Parts from Wild-Caught Fish, such as heads, bones, and viscera, are used as Byproducts for Fish Meal, Fish Oil, and Nutritional Supplments

label: Fish Byproducts (heads, bones, fins, vertebrae, cartilage) can be Harvested from Farmed Fish
title: Includes Fish Heads, Fins, and Vertebrae, rich in Collagen, minerals, and oils. Often used for Broth, Fish Meal, hydrolyzed protein, and pharmaceutical inputs

label: DMS (Dimethyl Sulfide) can be Degraded/Broken Down from Lignin (Wood)
title: DMS is formed through the chemical degradation of sulfur-containing compounds in Lignin, commonly occurring during the Kraft pulping of wood in paper production

label: DMS is a precursor of DMSO
title: DMSO is produced by chemically oxidizing DMS, a sulfur-containing compound.

label: Rooster Combs can be Harvested from Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls)
title: Rooster Combs can be harvested from farmed Poultry

label: Bacteria (Streptococcus spp. or Bacillus spp.) is a Primary Product of Biotechnology
title: In modern HA production, starter cultures of bacteria are maintained in biorepositories

label: Fish Meat & Products can be Harvested from Wild-Caught Fish
title: Fish Meat from Wild Caught Fish is harvested from natural marine and freshwater environments, commonly sourced from species such as Cod, Tuna, Anchovies, and Sardines

label: Fish Meat & Products can be Harvested from Farmed Fish
title: Fish Meat from Farmed Fish is sourced from aquaculture, commonly including species such as Salmon, Tilapia, Catfish, and Carp

label: Porcine Bones can be Harvested from Porcine (Pig)
title: Porcine Bones originate from farmed Pigs (Porcine) and are collected during slaughter and meat processing.

label: Corn is a Main Product of Corn Farming
title: Corn is harvested directly from the maize plant in Corn Farming

label: Avian (Poultry) Bones can be Harvested from Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls)
title: Avian Bones come from farmed Avian Species such as chickens, ducks, and turkeys, mainly as poultry processing byproducts.

label: Avian Skin can be Harvested from Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls)
title: Avian Skins are sourced from farmed Poultry such as Chickens and Ducks during Meat Processing.

label: Pig Skin can be Harvested from Porcine (Pig)
title: Pig Skin is sourced from farmed Pigs, widely utilised in Food Processing and Ingredient Manufacturing.

label: Porcine Connective Tissues (Tendons, Ligaments, and Fascia) can be Harvested from Porcine (Pig)
title: Porcine Connective Tissues are obtained from Pigs and collected as part of standard Meat-Processing byproduct streams.

label: Poultry Connective Tissues (Tendons, Ligaments, and Fascia) can be Harvested from Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls)
title: Avian Connective Tissues from Poultry are collected as bypoducts in the Poultry Industry for further uses, but with smaller quantities compared to Bovine and Porcine sources.

label: Eggshell Membrane can be Harvested from Eggs
title: Eggshell Membrane is linked to Laying Hens and the Egg Production Industry, sourced from Eggshells as a byproduct of the Egg Breaking Industry.

label: Animal Cartilage can be Harvested from Bovine (Cow)
title: Bovine animals are a major commercial source of Animal Cartilage, typically collected from the trachea, ribs, or sternum of cows and widely used for Collagen, Gelatin, and Chondroitin extraction.

label: Animal Cartilage can be Harvested from Porcine (Pig)
title: Porcine animals are another major source of Animal Cartilage, commonly harvested from the trachea, ribs, or joint tissues of pigs, alongside Bovine sources in supplement and food ingredient production.

label: Animal Cartilage can be Harvested from Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls)
title: Avian Cartilage is primarily sourced from farmed Poultry such as Chickens, Turkeys, and Ducks, typically from joints, the sternum, or the trachea, and is used to a lesser extent than Bovine or Porcine Cartilage.

label: Contain similar compositions
title: Shark Cartilage is a specific type of Animal Cartilage sourced from Sharks.

label: Shark Cartilage can be Harvested from Sharks and Elasmobranchs
title: Shark Cartilage is obtained from the skeletal structures and fins of Elasmobranchs, including sharks, rays, and skates. It is processed as a high-value material, mainly for niche Nutraceutical and Cosmetic applications.

label: Beef (Bovine Meat) can be Harvested from Bovine (Cow)
title: Beef is the meat derived from farmed Bovine animals, primarily cattle, and is one of the most widely consumed animal protein sources globally.

label: Pork (Pig Meat) can be Harvested from Porcine (Pig)
title: Pork is the primary meat product obtained from farmed Porcine animals (pigs) and is widely used across fresh, processed, and cured food products.

label: Poultry (Avian Meat) can be Harvested from Broiler Avian (Poultry/Fowls)
title: Poultry Meat—such as chicken, turkey, and duck—is harvested from farmed Avian species and represents a major global source of animal protein.

label: Bovine Bones can be Harvested from Bovine (Cow)
title: Bovine Bones are derived from farmed Bovine (Cows/Cattle), primarily as byproducts of the meat and dairy industries.
